masthead.dek: Big cats are the subject — but every answer is something you can reason over and recite: a country, a continent, one of the cats themselves, even a simple count. Narrow the field, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Seven questions across four enumerable answer sets — countries, continents, the cats themselves, and a count — sources noted per question.

dimensions: number of big cats that can roar
plan.note: Answer-routing design: the big cats are the SUBJECT, but each answer is drawn from an enumerable set the solver can walk — and the answer sets are deliberately varied (countries, continents, a U.S. state, a count) rather than locked on countries. This keeps an otherwise unbounded topic ('which animal...') fully guessable while staying fresh.

questions.q: Which country is home to more wild tigers than the rest of the world combined?
questions.detail: India holds over 70% of the world's wild tigers — about 3,680 of them in 2022.
questions.why: Decades of tiger reserves and anti-poaching patrols have pushed India's count back above 3,000 — more than every other tiger country put together.
questions.more: Russia's Far East is a distant second, with roughly 500–600 Siberian tigers.
questions.source: India National Tiger Estimation 2022 (~3,682 tigers; India holds >70% of the world's wild tigers).

questions.q: Wild cats are native to every inhabited continent but one. Which inhabited continent has none of its own?
questions.detail: Australia has no native cats at all; every wild cat there today descends from animals brought by settlers.
questions.why: Australia split off as its own landmass before cats spread around the globe, so pouched marsupials evolved to fill the hunter's role instead.
questions.more: Antarctica has no cats either, but it has no native land mammals of any kind.
questions.source: Zoogeography of Felidae — native to every continent except Australia and Antarctica.

questions.dimension: number of big cats that can roar
questions.q: Of all the big cats, only some can let out a true roar. How many can?
questions.detail: Four: the lion, tiger, leopard, and jaguar.
questions.why: A special stretchy ligament in the voice box gives these four their roar; the cheetah, cougar, and even the snow leopard lack it and purr or chirp instead.
questions.more: The snow leopard is a close cousin of the four roarers, but still can't manage a full roar.
questions.source: Felid vocal anatomy: the four roaring cats are lion, tiger, leopard, and jaguar.

questions.q: Most big cats hunt under cover of darkness. Which one is the odd one out, doing most of its hunting in broad daylight?
questions.detail: The cheetah — it hunts mainly by day, especially mid-morning and late afternoon.
questions.why: Hunting in daylight lets the cheetah use its eyesight and blistering speed, and keeps it clear of the bigger night-hunters — lions and hyenas — that would happily steal its kill or kill its cubs.
questions.more: The other big cats lean on stealth in low light, where their night vision gives them the edge.
questions.source: Smithsonian's National Zoo; Cheetah Conservation Fund (cheetahs are primarily diurnal hunters).

questions.q: Which country has more wild lions than any other?
questions.detail: Tanzania is home to roughly 14,500 lions — more than a third of all that remain.
questions.why: Vast protected lands like the Serengeti and Ruaha hold the biggest lion populations left on Earth.
questions.more: Most people guess South Africa or Kenya, but Tanzania is well out in front.
questions.source: WildAid (Tanzania's wild lion population ~14,500, the largest of any country).

questions.q: On which continent do more kinds of wild cat live than anywhere else?
questions.detail: Asia has the richest variety of wild cats — tigers, leopards, snow leopards, clouded leopards, and a long list of smaller forest cats.
questions.why: Its sweep of habitats, from steamy jungle to high cold mountains, gives more cat species room than any other continent.
questions.more: Africa is famous for its big cats, but counting every species — big and small — Asia comes out ahead.
questions.source: Wildcat Family and National Geographic species lists (Asia has the most wild cat species of any continent).

questions.q: The world's rarest big cat hangs on in the snowy forests of just one country's far east. Which country?
questions.detail: The Amur leopard — only about 100–130 left in the wild — survives mainly in Primorye, in Russia's Far East.
questions.why: It's the only leopard built for deep snow; a national park on the Russia–China border helped it recover from fewer than 30 animals in 2007.
questions.more: A few now slip across into neighboring China, the only other place it still roams.
questions.source: WWF; Land of the Leopard National Park (Amur leopard ~100–130, mainly SW Primorye, Russia).
